item.title: Re: Image Sourcing
item.content:encoded: ah, sorry for the confusion. I'll try to look more in deep for the sources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3641-Γ is the designation given to space exploration gear, technology, modules, vehicles, and technology that manifests at random within Burgh and Sands.

Brought to you by the Department of Redundancy Department.

item.title: JS Post - Image Sourcing
item.content:encoded: The image in this article comes from the United States Air Force, and is originally found here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly couldn't get into this, as much as I enjoy a few aspects; its too much of a mess.

This isn't due to the disjointed order of transmissions (that's a trope I'm particularly fond of), but the amount of different elements that are unceremoniously glossed over - none of which are developed to the point where they all gel together harmoniously.

First, it seems that the anomaly itself has too many moving parts that don't necessarily contribute to much of anything - they're a needless complexity. This could be written as simply transmissions from an undocumented mission, and it really wouldn't change much. I get that the ghosts have something to do with this "'Ahmed", but that too is another point just kinda lumped in with no strong relation to the overall plot.

This leads into my next point - Nased's motivations fluctuate between spite due to unrequited love, and a desire for a legacy or legitimacy of its personhood - neither of which feel concrete, topped with some spoopy revenge scheme, and the uncertainty in which everything plays out leaves it feeling really shallow.

I could see myself getting behind a more coherent version of this, but right now it's a strong -1 from me. It feels like it meanders through disparate plot points without really deciding what story its trying to tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have to be a special kind of genius to develop an AI that can get jealous

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Btw, Kant levels aren’t a thing. Kant Counters are devices used to measure Hume levels, it’s the name of the device not a separate measurement.

Thank you for telling me! It will be edited right away.

I'll keep this in mind for future articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s kind of a 2001 a Space Odyssey ripoff, but ultimately it was entertaining enough to upvote given the subversion of the AI’s motivation compared to HAL.

Btw, Kant levels aren’t a thing. Kant Counters are devices used to measure Hume levels, it’s the name of the device not a separate measurement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
especially their motivations.

I'll be sure to clear it up right now.

e: Edited Nased's first log. I hope it clears things up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is really interesting, but unfortunately, I felt like I didn't get a good sense of the characters, especially their motivations. The AI in particular. The anomaly is good though, and I like this part:

  • Dr. Kargard is ill.

Something about it makes me uneasy.

Edit: I looked at the change, but I'm still not really sure if that helps. I just don't think there's enough build up, and the characters still don't fully feel fleshed out. I'm keeping my novote for now.

Edit 2: I've reread it, and I like it much more now. The little changes were enough. Upvoted.

item.title: Re: Nice to See a UK-Based Scip
item.content:encoded: Correct! I wanted something revolving space to write about, and so I used the Solway Spaceman as inspiration. I'm glad you noticed. :-)

item.title: Nice to See a UK-Based Scip
item.content:encoded: Presume you selected Burgh by Sands in connection to the "Solway Spaceman" photograph.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
The third rendition of a story of a failed Foundation space mission, the ghosts of astronauts, Mars, an alien, and an AI who is denied of love and is, in turn, fueled by jealousy.

Thank you to Jack Evereds, Endren, and Golden506 for critique! And thank you for reading!
